channel.title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Food & Agriculture
channel.description: Curated OT/ICS advisories, incidents and threat intelligence. Editorial by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

item.title: [Critical] Ransomware reaches fairlife’s production systems — Coca-Cola halts US dairy manufacturing
item.description: The Coca-Cola Company disclosed on 16 July that a third party gained unauthorised access to part of its fairlife dairy subsidiary’s environment, including its production-related systems, in a ransomware event. US production is temporarily suspended; Canadian operations are unaffected, and the company says product quality and safety are not affected. No group has claimed the attack, Coca-Cola has not said whether data was taken, and it states the full scope and impacts are not yet known. This is the food and beverage sector’s clearest recent reminder that a plant does not need its PLCs touched to stop: ransomware rarely speaks ICS protocols — it lands on the ERP, MES, virtualisation and identity infrastructure that scheduling, batching, pasteurisation records and quality release depend on, and a perishable-product line halts anyway. - The Coca-Cola Company statement / fairlife / Cybersecurity Dive - Disclosed 16 Jul 2026 — US production suspended; investigation ongoing

item.title: [High] Cyberattack on Japan’s cold-chain giant Nichirei ripples through KFC and major retailers
item.description: Frozen-food and cold-storage leader Nichirei disconnected its systems on 13 July after detecting unauthorised server access, halting refrigerated-warehouse operations and Nichirei Foods shipping. The fallout spread fast across the supply chain: KFC Japan’s 1,300-plus restaurants faced Original Recipe ingredient-delivery disruption, and Aeon supermarkets, TableMark and Kura Sushi were among others affected. Some impacted servers held personal data; Nichirei filed an initial breach report and began phased restoration on 17 July. It has not confirmed whether a known ransomware or extortion group was involved. For food and beverage operators the lesson is about concentration risk: cold-chain and third-party logistics are single points of failure whose outage cascades into every downstream brand that depends on them — often faster than any of those brands can react. - Nichirei disclosure / SecurityWeek / The Record - Detected 13 Jul 2026 — restoration began 17 Jul; investigation ongoing

item.title: [Critical] SonicWall SMA 1000 zero-days exploited — the remote access into processing plants and cold chain
item.description: SonicWall confirmed active exploitation of two Secure Mobile Access 1000 flaws after investigating multiple cases. CVE-2026-15409 is a CVSS 10.0 unauthenticated server-side request forgery in the Work Place interface; CVE-2026-15410 (CVSS 7.2) lets an authenticated admin inject OS commands through the Appliance Management Console. CISA added both to the KEV catalog on 14 July with a 17 July federal deadline. For food and beverage the exposure follows the sector’s shape: processing and packaging sites are geographically dispersed, run lean overnight, and depend on remote support from OEMs for filling, packaging and refrigeration equipment. Cold-chain monitoring in particular is often reached remotely by a third party. When the appliance carrying that access is what gets exploited, the intrusion arrives as a legitimate maintenance session. - CVE-2026-15409 - SonicWall advisory & KEV addition 14 Jul 2026 — reporting 15 Jul; ongoing

item.title: [High] Lantronix serial-to-IP converter flaw exploited in the wild — the boxes bridging older process gear
item.description: Attackers are exploiting CVE-2025-67038 in Lantronix serial-to-IP converters, one of the flaws disclosed in April via the BRIDGE:BREAK research project. In food and beverage plants these devices are how legacy equipment reached the network without being replaced: weigh stations, older fillers and cappers, batch scales, refrigeration and pasteuriser controllers, and CIP skids. They were usually installed by an integrator during a retrofit, never entered in an asset register, and never patched, because the machine behind them still runs. That is what makes the category attractive — unmanaged, unpatched, inside the trusted network, with a direct path to a process asset. Exploitation is confirmed and follows an explicit OT threat warning; for most processors the honest first step is finding them. - CVE-2025-67038 - Disclosed Apr 2026 — exploitation reported 25 Jun 2026; ongoing

item.title: [High] Context: Food and Ag-ISAC’s 2025 data still frames the sector’s ransomware exposure
item.description: Foundational context for the incidents above rather than fresh news: the Food and Ag-ISAC’s ransomware report, published 12 February 2026, recorded 265 attacks against food and agriculture during 2025 — about 4.2% of the 6,377 incidents it tracked across all sectors, itself an 82% rise on the 3,508 tracked in 2024. Five groups — Qilin, Akira, CL0P, Play and Lynx — accounted for close to half of the sector’s recorded incidents. A follow-on sector threat report in March identified 72 active threat actors targeting food supply chains, with observed adversary activity attributed predominantly to Russia (about 59%) and China (about 25%). The numbers are a year old and should be read as baseline, not as this week’s picture. - Food and Ag-ISAC / Industrial Cyber - Ransomware report published 12 Feb 2026; sector threat report 17 Mar 2026 (2025 data)

item.title: [Critical] July ICS Patch Tuesday: critical Rockwell Logix DoS flaws and a Siemens CVSS 10 auth bypass
item.description: The July 2026 cycle carries real weight for food and beverage plants. Rockwell Automation published 12 advisories including three critical denial-of-service flaws in CompactLogix, ControlLogix, Compact GuardLogix and GuardLogix controllers that can force a major non-recoverable fault — a controller that stops and does not restart on its own. Allen-Bradley Logix hardware is widespread across filling, packaging and process lines, so this is a throughput and product-loss question, not a data one; on a line running perishable product, an unplanned controller stop has a cost that starts accruing immediately. Siemens published nine advisories, six critical, including a CVSS 10 authentication bypass in Opencenter X and fixes for Simatic S7-1500. Schneider fixed a high-severity code-execution flaw in its IGSS SCADA product. VDE CERT added five more covering Codesys, Wago, Murrelektronik and Mettler Toledo — the last of which is common in weighing and inspection. - Rockwell PSIRT / Siemens ProductCERT / Schneider / VDE CERT / SecurityWeek - Published 14 Jul 2026 (Patch Tuesday) — reporting 15 Jul

item.title: [High] BeyondTrust patches critical auth-bypass flaws in Remote Support and Privileged Remote Access
item.description: BeyondTrust disclosed two authentication-bypass vulnerabilities on 7 July affecting Remote Support (RS) and Privileged Remote Access (PRA). CVE-2026-40138 (CVSS 9.2) stems from improper validation of authentication data and could let a network-positioned attacker bypass access controls to reach the appliance, including elevated accounts; CVE-2026-40139 involves improper processing of RS authentication requests. Exploitation of both requires a specific authentication configuration to be enabled, narrowing the exposed population, and neither is under active exploitation — no public proof-of-concept exists. Cloud customers were patched on 21 April; self-hosted RS/PRA at 25.3.2 or earlier needs action. Alongside the SonicWall item, the theme is consistent: the tooling that brokers third-party access into plants is under sustained research pressure. - CVE-2026-40138 - Disclosed 7 Jul 2026

item.title: [Info] Australia’s CISC unveils Enhanced CIRMP Rules covering AI, legacy systems, supply chain and insider risk
item.description: Australia’s Cyber and Infrastructure Security Centre published Enhanced CIRMP Rules addressing AI, legacy systems, supply-chain and insider risks across critical infrastructure. Food and grocery sit within Australia’s SOCI critical-infrastructure regime, so processors and retailers with Australian operations have direct exposure here. The broader signal matters to everyone else: legacy systems and supply-chain risk are moving from acknowledged facts of life into written obligations. For a sector where a decades-old retort, filler or blast freezer is often described as unpatchable and left there, an explicit regulatory expectation changes what “accepted risk” has to look like on paper — and those expectations usually reach customer questionnaires long before they reach other jurisdictions’ statute books. - CISC / Industrial Cyber - Published 18 Jun 2026

item.title: [Info] NIST publishes an OT backup quick-start guide aimed at cutting recovery time
item.description: NIST released SP 1339, an OT Backup Quick Start Guide intended to improve industrial cyber resilience and accelerate incident recovery. It lands usefully next to this month’s Rockwell advisories describing faults a controller will not recover from unaided (see Critical Advisories). In a processing plant, what determines how fast you restart is PLC logic, HMI projects, recipe and setpoint data, batch records, drive parameters and the engineering-workstation image needed to push them back — not the file server. Those artefacts are frequently stale, undocumented, or sitting on the same domain as the systems they are supposed to restore. In food specifically there is a second clock running: extended downtime is not just lost output, it is product at risk and, potentially, a traceability gap. - NIST SP 1339 - Published 19 Jun 2026
